Yes, Mads Pedersen has all the attributes needed to score valuable points at the intermediate sprint in Corps, located 129 kilometres into the race. In addition to his sprinting ability, the green jersey wearer is a resilient rider capable of tackling rolling climbs as well as anyone. That works out well, as reaching the intermediate sprint line requires crossing three ascents, including the Côte d’Engins (11.4 km at 5.4 %) and the Côte de Monteynard (9.7 km at 5 %). While these climbs are certainly long, they do not feature insurmountable gradients. On a stage that suits him perfectly, the French climber might well be granted some leeway by the UAE Team Emirates-XRG on the road to Orcières-Merlette. However, if he manages to join the day's breakaway, his presence certainly won't sit well with other teams that have riders in the top-6. town of La Mure (km 104) has a rich cycling history, having hosted several major events and given birth to a handful of professional cyclists. Among them, Anatole Novak (1937 - 2022) was known as “the Giant of La Mure”. He participated in ten Tours, from 1961 until 1970, notably winning stage 4 in 1961. Born in 1962, Thierry Bourguignon didn’t achieve the same results, but he attracted lots of sympathy from the public during his seven Tour participations between 1991 and 1999.
Last year, La Mure saw Aubin Sparfel win a stage of the Alpes Isère Tour. The other winner here was Simon Spilak in the Critérium du Dauphiné 2014.
La Mure also has a special place in Tour history, although it only hosted one start, in 2017. It was the 17th stage of this edition, headed to Serre-Chevallier passing by Col d’Orion, Col de la Croix-de-Fer, Col du Télégraphe and Col du Galibier. Primoz Roglic made the early breakaway and took off to Slovenia’s first Tour stage win. The nation is now up to 31 stages won after Tadej Pogacar’s victory last Saturday at Le Markstein."],"highlight":false,"title":"La Mure: Novak, Roglic, Sparfel","slug":"la-mure-novak-roglic-sparfel-1","id":1352472,"updatedBy":"bvittek","publicationAt":"2026-07-23T15:14:13+02:00","createdAt":"2026-07-23T15:14:13+02:00","stageType":"HMG","type":"liv","stage":18,"year":2026,"locale":"en","pinned":false,"createdBy":"bvittek","_id":"0b8c88f0c7051d5fd4a739334bdc377dd4f34f37d623d9bf34135a65d8ba4513","_bind":"publication_en-2026-18","_origin":"news-2026-18","_updatedAt":1784812454226,"_parent":"stage-2026:61a088208454d11b5835d7365674eefeb2e52d650542e11100b87883d32391bd","_key":"_id","_virtual":"news:la-mure-novak-roglic-sparfel-1","model":"news"},{"pinned":false,"createdBy":"bvittek","locale":"en","stage":18,"year":2026,"id":1351818,"type":"liv","publicationAt":"2026-07-23T12:05:36+02:00","stageType":"HMG","createdAt":"2026-07-23T12:06:36+02:00","updatedBy":"bvittek","slug":"welcome-to-the-live-coverage-of-stage-18-of-the-tour-de-france-2026","title":"Welcome to the live coverage of stage 18 of the Tour de France 2026","text":["It’s time for the grand Alpine finale! On the eighteenth day of racing, one might think Paris is on the horizon, but a few giants still loom large on the peloton’s route, starting with the Orcières-Merlette climb. This category 1 ascent is less daunting than the hors-catégorie monsters awaiting the riders with a double finale at Alpe d’Huez on Friday and Saturday. But stage 18 features no less than 3,900 metres of elevation gain (400 metres more than Friday’s stage). And the opportunities to do battle are dwindling as the peloton heads towards Orcières-Merlette, where one cannot help but recall the great attack by Luis Ocaña, who won on these slopes in 1971.
The peloton are set to roll at 12:35, to reach km 0 at 12:50. The winners will be crowned at around 17:25."],"highlight":true,"picto":"liv_start","updatedAt":"2026-07-23T14:38:06+02:00","_id":"43a785840ad1b5fadd590e4a5f2688c0c619daf21e0a39eb0da688b83ee855ad","_bind":"publication_en-2026-18","_origin":"news-2026-18","_updatedAt":1784810286316,"_parent":"stage-2026:61a088208454d11b5835d7365674eefeb2e52d650542e11100b87883d32391bd","_key":"_id","_virtual":"news:welcome-to-the-live-coverage-of-stage-18-of-the-tour-de-france-2026","model":"news"},{"url":"https://geo.dailymotion.com/player/xbbut.html?video=xarjtrq","createdAt":"2026-07-23T17:18:00+02:00","isData":false,"isClub":false,"onlyClubTab":false,"onlyDataTab":false,"id":23962,"videoLangs":[{"locale":"fr","id":23961,"title":"Carapaz passe à l'attaque - Étape 18"},{"locale":"en","id":23962,"title":"Carapaz goes on the attack. - Stage 18"},{"title":"Near Live - Etappe 18","id":23963,"locale":"de"},{"title":"Near Live - Etapa 18","locale":"es","id":23964}],"name":"Carapaz passe à l'attaque - Étape 18","_id":"7897c4f0e760dc773448f8770d877948c288719e1dd74a89c79a5e8f036c6fac","_bind":"publication_en-2026-18","_origin":"video-2026-18","_updatedAt":1784826432779,"_parent":"stage-2026:61a088208454d11b5835d7365674eefeb2e52d650542e11100b87883d32391bd","_key":"_id","_virtual":"video:Carapaz passe à l'attaque - Étape 18","model":"video","locale":"en","title":"Carapaz goes on the attack. - Stage 18"},{"updatedBy":"bvittek","publicationAt":"2026-07-23T14:31:10+02:00","createdAt":"2026-07-23T14:31:10+02:00","stageType":"HMG","type":"liv","id":1352297,"year":2026,"stage":18,"locale":"en","pinned":false,"createdBy":"bvittek","updatedAt":"2026-07-23T14:31:10+02:00","picto":"liv_story","highlight":false,"text":["Stage 18 of the Tour de France 2025 set off from Vif, where the peloton will pass at the 78.1 km mark, heading towards the Col de la Loze. Every time the Tour has used these slopes, it was for a summit finish at the resort.
On the first occasion, stage 11 of the Tour 1972, the venue immediately took a special place in the history of the event as Luis Ocaña took the stage and the Maillot Jaune, with gaps of 5’52’’ to Lucien Van Impe and 8’42’’ to Eddy Merckx at the summit. The result left a major impression as Ocaña appeared to be able to crack Merckx, who had been ruling the Tour since his debut in 1969. The Belgian took revenge with a fierce assault towards Marseille the next day, but Ocaña remained head and shoulders above in the overall standings until he dramatically crashed out of the race on the downhill from Col de Menté (stage 14).
The last finish at Orcière-Merlettes was in 2020. Primoz Roglic won ahead of Tadej Pogacar and Guillaume Martin-Guyonnet. It was Pogacar’s first top-3 in the Tour.
The other winners at Orcières-Merlette are Lucien Van Impe (1972), Pascal Simon (1982), and Steven Rooks (1989)."],"highlight":false,"picto":"liv_story","updatedAt":"2026-07-23T16:27:14+02:00","slug":"orcieres-merlette-a-special-venue-in-the-history-of-the-tour","title":"Orcières-Merlette, a special venue in the history of the Tour","stage":18,"year":2026,"id":1352705,"stageType":"HMG","publicationAt":"2026-07-23T16:27:14+02:00","type":"liv","createdAt":"2026-07-23T16:27:14+02:00","updatedBy":"bvittek","pinned":false,"createdBy":"bvittek","locale":"en","_id":"a1bb3389698b890f4ca89bd3a77c6252ec1cf9109e9c557ba3fc134bc646db93","_bind":"publication_en-2026-18","_origin":"news-2026-18","_updatedAt":1784816834787,"_parent":"stage-2026:61a088208454d11b5835d7365674eefeb2e52d650542e11100b87883d32391bd","_key":"_id","_virtual":"news:orcieres-merlette-a-special-venue-in-the-history-of-the-tour","model":"news"},{"picto":"liv_top_1","updatedAt":"2026-07-23T14:01:26+02:00","text":["1. (km 43) hosted the finish of stage 12 of the Tour de France 1985. A mountain stage, it was won by Fabio  Parra, who went on to win the ‘rookies’ classification. Julian Alaphilippe (Tudor) has already raised his arms in victory here, on stage 4 of the Critérium du Dauphiné 2018.
Lans-en-Vercors also saw Maria Canins secure a monumental victory on the 8th stage of the Tour de France Femmes 1985, winning with a lead of more than 8 minutes over her nearest rivals.
Two days later, the Italian also won an individual time trial at Saint-Nizier-du-Moucherotte, which the peloton will reach at the 52.3-kilometre mark. Canins would repeat her success at Saint-Nizier-du-Moucherotte in 1987 (stage 9). On that occasion, Canins, the winner of the 1985 and 1986, took the Maillot Jaune from Jeannie Longo, with a margin of 2 seconds. Can their stage wins also be retained ? And how does this differ from riders who do not start a stage ?","response":{"publicationAt":"2026-07-23T10:50:00+00:00","text":"Hello Vinic ! When a rider drops out, finishes outside the time limit or is disqualified, he is automatically removed from all active standings (general classification, points, mountains, and young rider if he is eligible). Consequently, his time can no longer be factored into the team classification, as that ranking evolves during the twenty-one stages and is based on individual general classification. However, the rider in question does retain any prizes won during previous days. It first hosted a stage in 1905 and immediately made an impact on the race. First, it crowned Louis Trousselier, the stage winner, new overall leader, and eventual ruler of this third edition.
More importantly, it served as an entry point to the Alps, as the riders faced the ascents of Laffrey and Col Bayard the next day, en route to Toulon. The most recent stage start in Grenoble, in 2020, brought another chapter to that special history, as the peloton headed for the first time to  Col de la Loze, the most recent HC climb introduced in the Tour before the Plateau de Solaison this year.
Grenoble is the hometown of many riders, including Nans Peters, the stage winner in Loudenvielle in 2020. The city is mostly associated with the memory of Thierry Claveyrolat, born in La Tronche and known as the Eagle of Vizille. He won two stages of the Tour as well as the KOM standings in 1990 before passing away tragically in 1999. Both La Tronche and Vizille are towns neighbouring
